title: Simón Bolívar
summary: The Venezuelan-born military commander and political leader who liberated most of Spanish South America between 1813 and 1825, governing as president or dictator across Venezuela, Colombia, Ecuador, Peru, and Bolivia — and who spent his final years watching the republics he had created fragment, federate against him, and slide toward the caudillo instability that would define Latin American politics for generations.
hook: Simón Bolívar liberated six nations and governed none of them successfully. He spent the last decade of his life attempting to hold together a Gran Colombia that was already disintegrating, issuing constitutions that were ignored, assuming dictatorial powers he justified as temporary, and watching the republican ideal he had fought for become, in practice, a competition among regional strongmen. He died in 1830, en route to exile, having resigned the presidency of a country that no longer existed. He is remembered across South America as El Libertador. The question of what he actually liberated — and for whom — has never been settled.

beginner.theFigure:
Simón Bolívar was born in 1783 in Caracas, in what was then the Spanish colony of Venezuela. He came from a wealthy creole family — Spanish descent, born in the Americas — and was educated partly in Europe, where he encountered Enlightenment ideas about liberty and republican government. He returned to South America and became the leading military and political figure of the independence movements that swept the continent between 1810 and 1825.

By the time he was done, territories that became Venezuela, Colombia, Ecuador, Panama, Peru, and Bolivia had broken from Spanish rule. No single person had liberated more territory in the history of the Western Hemisphere. He is called El Libertador — the Liberator — across South America, and his image appears on currency, in public squares, and in national pantheons from Caracas to Lima.
beginner.worldInherited:
The Spanish colonial system had governed South America for three centuries. It placed power in the hands of peninsulares — Spaniards born in Spain — while creoles born in the Americas were excluded from the highest offices regardless of wealth or education. Indigenous peoples and enslaved Africans were at the bottom of a rigid racial hierarchy.

The system began to crack when Napoleon invaded Spain in 1808 and removed the Spanish king. Spanish colonial authorities lost their source of legitimacy. Local governing juntas formed across the Americas. Some were loyal to Spain; others pushed toward independence. Bolívar emerged from this crisis as the man who could translate military victories into a continental political project.
beginner.howExercisedPower:
Bolívar's military campaigns were among the most extraordinary in the age of revolution. He crossed the Andes with a ragged army in 1819 — over passes that experienced soldiers thought impassable — and won the Battle of Boyacá, liberating New Granada. He won the Battle of Carabobo in 1821, securing Venezuela. He sent Antonio José de Sucre to win the Battle of Pichincha in 1822, liberating Ecuador. He entered Lima in 1823 and, with Sucre, defeated the last major Spanish forces at Ayacucho in 1824.

Politically, Bolívar wanted a unified republic — Gran Colombia — encompassing Venezuela, Colombia, and Ecuador, with the possibility of broader federation across the continent. He wrote constitutions, served as president, and when constitutions failed, assumed emergency powers. He believed that South America was not ready for the liberal democracy of the United States model — that it needed strong executive authority to hold fractious societies together.
beginner.whatTheyChanged:
Spanish colonial rule in South America ended. The independence that Bolívar won was real. The political order that replaced it was far from what he intended. Gran Colombia fragmented into Venezuela, Colombia, and Ecuador within months of his death. Peru and Bolivia went their own ways. The regional strongmen — caudillos — who had been useful military commanders became the dominant political force across the continent.

The independence movements had been led by creoles — the Spanish-descended elite born in the Americas. Indigenous peoples and enslaved Africans had fought on both sides and received little. Slavery was abolished gradually and incompletely across the liberated territories. Land and power remained concentrated in the same creole families that had held them under Spanish rule, now without Spanish oversight.
beginner.legendVsRecord:
The Bolívar of the continental pantheon is a pure liberator — the man who freed South America from Spanish tyranny and gave it republican government. The historical Bolívar is more complicated. He assumed dictatorial powers in 1828 and survived an assassination attempt he blamed on his political rival Francisco de Paula Santander. He proposed a lifetime presidency in his Bolivian constitution — a provision that was rejected almost everywhere it was offered. He died certain that his life's work had failed.

The myth erases his authoritarianism and his racial politics. Bolívar was a creole who spoke of liberty but governed through emergency powers. He freed enslaved people who fought for independence but did not abolish slavery systematically. His vision of Gran Colombia was a centralized republic led by enlightened creole leadership — not the participatory democracy the term 'republic' implied to his European contemporaries.

general.theFigure:
John Lynch's Simón Bolívar: A Life is the essential scholarly biography — comprehensive, analytically rigorous, and honest about both the achievement and the failure. Lynch situates Bolívar within the structural constraints of his moment: the weakness of creole institutions, the fragmentation of territory, the lack of a literate political class, and the persistent loyalty of much of the population to local rather than national identity. His conclusion is that Bolívar's failure was not personal but structural — the conditions for stable republican government did not exist in the societies he liberated.

Marie Arana's Bolívar: American Liberator provides the most readable single-volume account — attentive to personality and narrative in ways that Lynch's more analytical account is not. The two books are complementary rather than competing.
general.worldInherited:
The Haitian Revolution of 1791-1804 shaped Bolívar's political calculations in ways that are rarely acknowledged in the canonical accounts. Haiti had produced the only successful slave revolution in the Western Hemisphere, and its president Alexandre Pétion provided Bolívar with ships, arms, and soldiers in 1816 in exchange for a promise to free enslaved people in liberated territories. Bolívar honored the promise partially and inconsistently. His fear of a Haitian-style social revolution — a war of race as well as class — constrained his willingness to mobilize the enslaved and indigenous populations whose numbers made them the majority of the continent he claimed to be liberating.

Frank Safford and Marco Palacios's Colombia: Fragmented Land, Divided Society is essential for understanding the structural conditions that defeated Bolívar's project in New Granada specifically — the geographic fragmentation, the regional economic divergence, and the absence of the social infrastructure that held other republics together.
general.howExercisedPower:
Bolívar's 1826 Bolivian Constitution — his most considered statement of political theory — proposed a lifetime president with the power to name his own successor, a hereditary senate, and a highly restricted franchise. It was rejected by Bolivia within a year and never seriously implemented elsewhere. It reveals the depth of his pessimism about popular self-governance: he believed that South American societies, shaped by three centuries of Spanish despotism, could not sustain liberal republican institutions without a strong executive to hold them together during a transitional period of indefinite length.

Germán Carrera Damas's El culto a Bolívar argues that the Bolívar myth was constructed after his death by Venezuelan elites who needed a founding figure to legitimate their political authority — and that the myth systematically distorted the historical record to serve those political purposes. Carrera Damas's account is a foundational text in the historiography of Latin American nationalism and a devastating analysis of how founding myths function politically.
general.whatTheyChanged:
The independence movements created states without nations. The territories Bolívar liberated had colonial administrative boundaries but no shared national identity, no unified economic space, and no political tradition of self-governance. The creole elite that inherited power had been trained to administer a colonial system, not to build a republic. The result was that the formal structures of republican government — constitutions, elections, congresses — coexisted with the informal reality of military strongmen and regional power brokers who made the actual political decisions.

The caudillo system that emerged from the independence period was not simply a failure to implement republican ideals. It was a political form adapted to the actual social conditions: personal loyalty to a military leader, regional economic networks, and the absence of the impersonal state institutions that European republics had built over centuries. Understanding caudillismo as an adaptation rather than simply a pathology is the most important revision in 20th-century Latin American historiography.
general.legendVsRecord:
The question of Bolívar's racial politics is the most contested dimension of his legacy. He was born into a slave-owning family and owned enslaved people himself. He freed enslaved soldiers who fought for independence. He promised Pétion to free the enslaved in liberated territories and honored that promise inconsistently. He spoke of racial equality in the abstract and maintained a political coalition that depended on creole elite support.

David Bushnell's The Liberator's Legacy offers the most nuanced assessment: Bolívar's racial politics were contradictory in ways that reflected the contradictions of his class position rather than simple hypocrisy. He genuinely believed in the principles he articulated; he also genuinely depended on social structures that those principles would have undermined. This contradiction — between the liberator's ideals and the liberator's social base — is the central unresolved problem in Bolívar studies.

advanced.theFigure:
The most important analytical question about Bolívar is whether his political failures were contingent — the product of specific miscalculations and personal limitations — or structural — the product of social conditions that made his project impossible regardless of what he did. Lynch's structural account is the dominant scholarly position: the absence of a literate political class, the fragmentation of territory, the weakness of markets that might have created cross-regional economic integration, and the loyalty of populations to local identity rather than national — all of these made Gran Colombia's fragmentation likely regardless of who governed it.

The contingency arguments focus on specific decisions: Bolívar's choice to concentrate power in himself rather than building institutional alternatives; his failure to incorporate the llaneros — the mixed-race plainsmen who were his best soldiers — into the political order he was building; his underestimation of Santander's institutional base in New Granada. These arguments are not refuted by Lynch's structural account but they are subordinated to it. The question of how much a specific leader can overcome structural constraints is genuinely unresolvable in general; the Bolívar case is one of the better test cases.
advanced.worldInherited:
The comparison with the North American founding is unavoidable and has generated an extensive scholarly literature. The standard account holds that the United States succeeded where Spanish America failed because of cultural and institutional inheritance — British legal traditions, Protestantism, the colonial experience of self-governance. The Spanish colonies had autocratic governance, Catholic ecclesiastical hierarchy, and no tradition of representative institutions.

This culturalist account has been challenged on multiple fronts. It underweights the role of geography and economic structure — the United States had a more integrated coastal economy that created cross-regional interests in union; Spanish American territories were separated by mountain ranges and jungle that created powerful centrifugal forces. It also underweights the role of race — the United States resolved the contradiction between liberty and slavery by maintaining slavery, while Bolívar's movements were more racially complex and the social contradictions therefore harder to contain. The comparison is productive precisely because it resists easy resolution.
advanced.howExercisedPower:
Bolívar's relationship to the Haitian model is the most analytically productive lens for understanding his political conservatism. He had witnessed the Haitian Revolution's trajectory: slave revolt, racial warfare, the massacre of the white population, and the emergence of a state that the Atlantic world refused to recognize. He needed Pétion's support; he also feared that mobilizing the enslaved and indigenous populations of South America would produce a Haitian outcome — a social revolution that the creole elite could not control and that he could not govern.

This fear shaped his military strategy — he preferred to use creole and llanero forces rather than mass enslaved mobilization — and his political program — he offered gradual, limited emancipation rather than immediate abolition. Whether this was realism about the limits of the possible or a betrayal of the principles he articulated is the central moral question in the Bolívar literature. The answer depends partly on whether one believes a more radical program was available and viable, which is ultimately a counterfactual judgment.
advanced.whatTheyChanged:
The question of what Latin American independence actually changed for the majority of the population — indigenous peoples, enslaved Africans and their descendants, mixed-race communities — is the central revision in contemporary Latin American historiography. The older nationalist historiography, which Carrera Damas's work helped dismantle, presented independence as a liberation for all. The newer social history, drawing on the work of scholars like Marixa Lasso and Marcela Echeverri, shows that for much of the population the transition from colonial to republican rule changed the legal framework while maintaining or worsening material conditions.

For indigenous communities, the abolition of indigenous tribute — a colonial tax — also abolished the legal protections that tribute status had provided. Republican governments were more aggressive about privatizing communal indigenous lands than the Spanish had been, because republican ideology of individual property rights was incompatible with communal land tenure. The liberal republic, in this reading, was worse for indigenous communities than the colonial system it replaced.
advanced.legendVsRecord:
The political use of Bolívar's legacy in contemporary Latin America reveals the flexibility of founding myths. Hugo Chávez's Bolivarian Revolution in Venezuela claimed Bolívar for socialist anti-imperialism — emphasizing his hostility to the United States (his 1829 comment that the United States 'seems destined by Providence to plague America with misery in the name of liberty') and his pan-American vision. The Venezuelan right claimed Bolívar for constitutional republicanism and opposition to authoritarianism. Both claims have textual support; neither is historically complete.

The pattern is general: founding figures whose actual politics were complex and contradictory become available for whatever political uses the present requires, precisely because the myth has suppressed the complexity. Carrera Damas's argument about the construction of the Bolívar cult is now itself a political resource — invoked by critics of Chavismo to argue that the Bolivarian Revolution is a manufactured legitimation strategy rather than a genuine inheritance. The historiography of Bolívar is inseparable from the politics of contemporary Venezuela, which makes objective assessment unusually difficult.

foundation.theStory: Bolívar matters as the figure who makes legible the gap between political liberation and social transformation — between ending colonial rule and changing the social order that colonialism had created. He also matters as the foundational figure of Latin American nationalism and the subject of the most politically contested founding myth in the Western Hemisphere.
foundation.theDebate: The central debates concern whether Bolívar's failures were contingent or structural; what his racial politics reveal about the limits of creole republicanism; how to assess the independence movements' consequences for indigenous and enslaved populations; and how the Bolívar myth has been constructed and deployed across two centuries of Latin American politics.
foundation.whyItMatters: Bolívar matters for understanding Latin American independence, the caudillo political tradition, the relationship between military and civilian power in republican governments, pan-American political thought, and the historiography of nationalist mythology.
questions: Bolívar believed that South America needed a strong executive — essentially a lifetime president — to hold fractious societies together during the transition from colonial rule to republican government. He called this 'enlightened despotism' in republican form. Was he right about the social conditions he faced, and wrong only about the solution — or was the solution itself the problem, since executive concentration of power tended to produce the caudillo instability he was trying to prevent?

The Bolívar myth was constructed after his death by elites who needed a founding figure. The historical Bolívar died a failure, convinced his life's work had been undone. What does the gap between the historical figure and the national myth tell us about how founding myths function — and what do they require us to forget about the actual founding?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: Lynch's Simón Bolívar: A Life is the essential scholarly biography. Arana's Bolívar: American Liberator is the essential readable account. Carrera Damas's El culto a Bolívar is the essential critique of the myth. Bushnell's The Liberator's Legacy provides the most nuanced assessment of the racial politics. Lasso's Myths of Harmony: Race and Republicanism during the Age of Revolution examines the racial dimension of the independence movements in detail.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: The documentary record for Bolívar is extensive — his writings, letters, and constitutions are well-preserved and have been carefully edited. The main limits are: the perspective of indigenous and enslaved populations is poorly documented relative to the creole elite; the internal decision-making of military campaigns is reconstructed from after-the-fact accounts; and the counterfactual question of what a more radical social program would have produced cannot be answered from the evidence.
interpretation.takingAPosition: A defensible scholarly position on Bolívar must hold the achievement and the failure together without collapsing either into the other. The strongest position holds that Bolívar accomplished something genuinely remarkable — the military liberation of most of Spanish South America — under conditions that made his political project structurally unlikely to succeed. The social conditions for stable republican government — literate political class, integrated economy, impersonal state institutions — did not exist in the territories he liberated, and he could not create them through constitutional design or force of will. The limitations of his racial politics were real and consequential; they also reflected the contradictions of his class position rather than simple bad faith. The myth that replaced him serves purposes he would not have recognized as his own.
questions: Lynch argues that Bolívar's failure was structural rather than personal — that the social conditions for stable republican government did not exist in the territories he liberated. If this is right, then Bolívar's specific decisions mattered less than the structural constraints he operated within. Does this analysis apply to other liberation movements — and if so, what does it imply about what political leaders can and cannot accomplish through force of will and constitutional design?

Contemporary Venezuelan politics has claimed Bolívar for both socialist revolution (Chavismo) and liberal republicanism (the opposition). Both claims have textual support. What does it tell us about the nature of founding myths that a single historical figure can be plausibly claimed by political movements with incompatible programs — and what should historians do when the historical record is actively mobilized for contemporary political purposes?

research.topic: The structural causes of Gran Colombia's fragmentation
research.content: The scholarly consensus holds that Gran Colombia's fragmentation into Venezuela, Colombia, and Ecuador was driven by structural factors rather than Bolívar's personal failures: geographic separation by the Andes created distinct regional economies with divergent interests; the absence of cross-regional economic integration meant there was no material basis for political union; and the creole elite's primary loyalty was regional rather than national. Lynch's account is the most comprehensive statement of this position, and it is broadly accepted by specialists in the period.

research.topic: The independence movements' consequences for indigenous communities
research.content: Emerging scholarship, drawing on regional archives and indigenous community records, has substantially revised the account of what independence meant for indigenous populations. Scholars including Marcela Echeverri and Sinclair Thomson have shown that for many indigenous communities, the transition from colonial to republican rule was materially harmful — the abolition of colonial legal protections combined with republican privatization of communal lands produced dispossession that the colonial system had partially prevented. This revision challenges nationalist narratives of independence as universal liberation.

research.topic: Bolívar's internal reasoning about emancipation and racial politics
research.content: Bolívar's private reasoning about the limits of emancipation — why he honored his promise to Pétion inconsistently, how he weighed the risk of social revolution against the military utility of mobilizing enslaved populations — is not fully recoverable from the documentary record. His letters and speeches present his public position; the internal deliberation is absent. What can be established: the pattern of his decisions, the constraints he faced, and the outcomes his choices produced. What cannot be recovered: whether he believed his inconsistent emancipation policy was morally adequate or a pragmatic compromise he was uncomfortable with.

comparativeNarrative.name: El Libertador
comparativeNarrative.content: The dominant Latin American tradition presents Bolívar as the continental liberator — the figure who ended Spanish colonialism and gave South America its independence and its republican identity. This tradition, which Carrera Damas showed was systematically constructed by 19th-century Venezuelan elites, emphasizes Bolívar's military genius, his pan-American vision, and his selfless dedication to liberty. It suppresses the authoritarianism, the racial contradictions, and the failure of his political project. The tradition remains politically powerful — Chávez invoked it; so did his opponents — which makes critical engagement with it politically charged in ways that affect historical scholarship.

comparativeNarrative.name: Liberation That Wasn't
comparativeNarrative.content: From the perspective of indigenous and Afro-Latin American communities, the independence movements delivered political change at the top of the social order without transforming the bottom. Creole elites replaced Spanish administrators; enslaved people remained enslaved or were freed into poverty; indigenous communities lost colonial legal protections without gaining republican ones. This tradition does not deny that Spanish colonialism was oppressive — it argues that creole independence was a transfer of power within the elite, not a liberation of those the elite governed.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Failed Republic
comparativeNarrative.content: The North Atlantic liberal tradition — shaped by the comparison with the United States — tends to read Bolívar's political failure as evidence of cultural or institutional deficiency in Spanish American societies: insufficient Protestantism, insufficient experience of self-governance, insufficient individualism. This tradition underweights the structural factors (geography, economic fragmentation) and overweights the cultural ones, and has been substantially challenged by historians who argue that it projects Anglo-American conditions onto a fundamentally different social context.

rabbitHole.label: The Haitian Revolution
rabbitHole.reason: Bolívar received ships, arms, and soldiers from Haitian president Pétion in exchange for a promise to free enslaved people in liberated territories. The Haitian Revolution shaped his fear of social revolution and constrained his emancipation politics throughout his campaigns.

rabbitHole.label: Decolonization
rabbitHole.reason: The South American independence movements are the 19th-century precursor to 20th-century decolonization — and share its central paradox: political liberation achieved by an educated elite that inherits the colonial social structure rather than dismantling it.

rabbitHole.label: The American Revolution
rabbitHole.reason: Bolívar explicitly compared his project to the American founding and explicitly rejected the American model as unsuited to South American conditions. The comparison between the two revolutions — why one produced stable republican government and the other did not — is the foundational question in the historiography of both.

rabbitHole.label: Nelson Mandela
rabbitHole.reason: Both Bolívar and Mandela achieved political liberation while leaving the economic structures of the previous order largely intact. The comparison across different centuries and continents illuminates what political liberation can accomplish and what it characteristically fails to transform.

rabbitHole.label: Nationalism
rabbitHole.reason: The Latin American independence movements created states that then had to create nations — inventing national identities for territories whose populations had no pre-existing sense of shared national belonging. Bolívar's Gran Colombia failed partly because the national identity it required did not exist.

rabbitHole.label: Caudillismo
rabbitHole.reason: The caudillo system — military strongmen governing through personal loyalty rather than institutional authority — was the political form that emerged from the independence period's wreckage. Understanding caudillismo is essential to understanding why Bolívar's republican project failed and what replaced it.

rabbitHole.label: The Bolivarian Revolution
rabbitHole.reason: Hugo Chávez's Bolivarian Revolution claimed Bolívar's legacy for 21st-century socialist politics — a political appropriation that reveals how founding myths function and what they require historical figures to have been, regardless of what they actually were.

rabbitHole.label: Republicanism
rabbitHole.reason: Bolívar was a republican by formation and conviction who concluded that classical republican institutions were unsuited to the societies he governed — and who attempted to design a hybrid between republican legitimacy and executive authority. His political theory is one of the most sustained engagements with republicanism's limits in the Atlantic world.

reference.author: Lynch, John
reference.title: Simón Bolívar: A Life
reference.annotation: The essential scholarly biography — comprehensive, analytically rigorous, and the definitive statement of the structural interpretation of Bolívar's political failures.

reference.author: Arana, Marie
reference.title: Bolívar: American Liberator
reference.annotation: The most readable single-volume account — attentive to personality, narrative, and the human texture of the campaigns in ways that Lynch's more analytical account is not.

reference.author: Carrera Damas, Germán
reference.title: El culto a Bolívar
reference.annotation: The foundational deconstruction of the Bolívar myth — shows how the legend was systematically constructed by Venezuelan elites after his death to serve political purposes he would not have recognized.

reference.author: Bushnell, David
reference.title: The Liberator's Legacy
reference.annotation: The most nuanced assessment of Bolívar's racial politics — argues that his contradictions reflected his class position rather than simple hypocrisy.

reference.author: Lasso, Marixa
reference.title: Myths of Harmony: Race and Republicanism during the Age of Revolution, Colombia 1795–1831
reference.annotation: Essential for the racial dimension of the independence movements — examines how republican ideology of racial harmony coexisted with persistent racial hierarchy in practice.

commerce.author: Arana, Marie
commerce.title: Bolívar: American Liberator
commerce.note: Start here — Arana writes with narrative energy and human scale; the best entry point for someone who wants to understand why Bolívar matters before engaging the scholarly debates.

commerce.author: Lynch, John
commerce.title: Simón Bolívar: A Life
commerce.note: For the full scholarly account — Lynch is demanding but authoritative, the book you need if you want to understand what the historiography actually says.

commerce.author: Bushnell, David
commerce.title: The Liberator's Legacy
commerce.note: For the racial politics specifically — the most honest engagement with the gap between Bolívar's ideals and his practice.

commerce.author: Lasso, Marixa
commerce.title: Myths of Harmony
commerce.note: For the independence movements' consequences for Afro-Colombian and indigenous communities — the essential revision of the nationalist narrative.

commerce.author: García Márquez, Gabriel
commerce.title: The General in His Labyrinth
commerce.note: García Márquez's novel of Bolívar's final river journey — not biography but the most powerful imaginative engagement with what his failure felt like from the inside.

popularCulture.title: The General in His Labyrinth — Gabriel García Márquez (1989)
popularCulture.description: Bolívar's final river journey to exile, rendered in García Márquez's late style. Gets right the psychological texture of failure — the man who liberated a continent watching his political creation dissolve. Gets deliberately selective: it is not biography but elegy, interested in the feeling of the end rather than the causes of the failure.

popularCulture.title: Bolivarianism as contemporary political vocabulary
popularCulture.description: Hugo Chávez named his political movement the Bolivarian Revolution, turning a 19th-century independence leader into the patron of 21st-century anti-American socialism. The appropriation required selective reading: emphasizing Bolívar's hostility to the United States and his pan-American vision while suppressing his authoritarianism. The Venezuelan opposition claimed the same Bolívar for constitutional republicanism. The gap between the myth and the history it claims is the political story.

popularCulture.title: El Libertador as title and archetype
popularCulture.description: The title 'Liberator' has been applied to political and military leaders across Latin America in ways that reveal its ideological function: it frames political change as liberation from external oppression rather than as internal social transformation. The archetype obscures the question of who actually benefited from the liberation — which in Bolívar's case is more complicated than the title implies.

popularCulture.title: Bolívar: Una lucha admirable (Netflix, 2019)
popularCulture.description: Colombian-produced drama covering Bolívar's life from youth through independence. Gets right the scale and violence of the campaigns, the geographic enormity of the independence project. Gets limited by the nationalist framework that made it possible to produce: it reproduces the heroic myth more than it interrogates it, and treats the creole independence project as straightforwardly representing the people of South America.

popularCulture.title: The Bolívar currency and the petrodollar
popularCulture.description: Venezuela named its currency the bolívar in 1879, later redenominated as the bolívar fuerte and then the bolívar soberano as inflation destroyed its value. The currency that bore the Liberator's name became worthless under the Bolivarian Revolution — an irony that the opposition deploys regularly. The debasement of the currency named after Bolívar tracks the debasement of his political legacy in contemporary Venezuelan politics.
